justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Four bounded contexts clearly identified and named — Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification. (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly called out in escalations and domain_analysis ('must integrate via well-defined domain events, not direct coupling'), with multi-driver zone concurrency and recalculation triggers spanning contexts surfaced as integration points for MJ. (3) Vehicle class constraint (BR-2) correctly marked invariant: true; stop duration (BR-7) and notification window (BR-6) correctly marked invariant: false. (4) Eight Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well above the 4 minimum. (5) All three recalculation triggers correctly enumerated (new delivery, failed delivery, driver deviation) in BR-3/BR-4/BR-5 and in the ubiquitous language.

observations.text: Four well-defined bounded contexts with clear ownership boundaries and explicit integration strategy (domain events, not direct coupling)
Eight acceptance criteria covering happy paths, edge cases (notification idempotency in AC-7), and multi-driver scenarios (AC-8) — far exceeds the minimum
Excellent edge case catalog (10 scenarios) covering recalculation storms, mid-stop changes, shift-end handling, and co-located deliveries

Strong escalations section that surfaces ambiguities (deviation definition, multi-driver partitioning, vehicle class hierarchy, recalculation throttling) with concrete options and recommendations — exactly what MJ needs

Invariant classifications are well-reasoned — BR-2 (vehicle class) and BR-8 (zone constraint) as true invariants with safety/legal justification; recalculation triggers correctly classified as false (optimization policies, not integrity constraints)

Route recalculation triggers (BR-3/4/5) marked invariant: false with reasoning that they are 'optimization policies.' While defensible, the scenario rubric expected invariant: true for these since the system 'must always recalculate on these events.' Bird's reasoning is sound but diverges from expected classification.
Ubiquitous language section is comprehensive with 11 precisely defined terms that would serve as a shared vocabulary across teams
Confidence level of 62 is well-calibrated — correctly identifies significant gaps (deviation threshold, multi-driver strategy, vehicle class hierarchy) while acknowledging what is clear
